query: What's the difference between acute and chronic pain?

chunks.chunk_text:
# Recognizing Acute vs. Chronic Pain
## How the Two Differ in Practice
The most useful distinctions are timing, trigger, and course. Acute pain comes on suddenly, ties to a specific event, and fades as healing progresses. Chronic pain builds or persists over a much longer window, may lack an obvious cause, and does not reliably improve with time alone. Acute pain that does not resolve as expected can transition into chronic pain, though this is not inevitable. Recognizing which pattern a lifter describes helps determine the response: an acute strain often settles with conservative early care, whereas pain that has outlasted normal healing time signals the need for a clinician's assessment rather than continued self-management.
chunks.source_citation: Acute vs. Chronic Pain: Key Differences, Cleveland Clinic — https://health.clevelandclinic.org/acute-vs-chronic-pain

chunks.chunk_text:
# Recognizing Acute vs. Chronic Pain
## What Acute Pain Is
Acute pain starts quickly and typically resolves once the underlying issue is treated or has healed. It has a specific, identifiable trigger, such as a broken bone, a burn, surgery, dental work, or a training injury like a muscle strain. In this sense acute pain works as a protective warning signal, comparable to a loud alarm that draws attention to a problem that needs addressing. For a lifter, sharp pain that appears during or immediately after a specific set is usually acute. It signals that something was loaded beyond what the tissue could tolerate, and it warrants stopping the movement rather than pushing through.
chunks.source_citation: Acute vs. Chronic Pain: Key Differences, Cleveland Clinic — https://health.clevelandclinic.org/acute-vs-chronic-pain

chunks.chunk_text:
# Recognizing Acute vs. Chronic Pain
## What Chronic Pain Is
Chronic pain is ongoing and generally persists longer than three to six months. Unlike acute pain, it can continue even after the original injury appears to have healed, because the nervous system keeps sending pain signals, comparable to an alarm that keeps ringing after the emergency has passed. Chronic pain may follow an injury that did not recover as expected, or it may accompany conditions such as arthritis. Sometimes no clear cause can be identified. For a recreational lifter, pain that lingers for months, recurs with the same movements, or has no obvious single injury behind it fits this pattern and should be evaluated professionally.
chunks.source_citation: Acute vs. Chronic Pain: Key Differences, Cleveland Clinic — https://health.clevelandclinic.org/acute-vs-chronic-pain

chunks.chunk_text:
# Recognizing Acute vs. Chronic Pain
## Conservative Early Response to Acute Pain
For a fresh, minor acute injury such as a mild strain or sprain, first-line management is conservative and centers on protecting the area and letting it settle. The RICE approach, rest, ice, compression, and elevation, is commonly used for these injuries, alongside over-the-counter pain relief where appropriate. This guidance applies to minor cases and is not a substitute for evaluation. Severe pain, an inability to use the limb, deformity, or pain that fails to improve over a reasonable period all point toward professional care. An AI coach should treat conservative rest and de-loading as the default response to new acute pain, never encouraging a lifter to train through it.
chunks.source_citation: Acute vs. Chronic Pain: Key Differences, Cleveland Clinic — https://health.clevelandclinic.org/acute-vs-chronic-pain

chunks.chunk_text:
# Exercise Selection for Common Goals
## When to Change Exercises
Exercise substitution is warranted when: a lift causes pain beyond normal muscle fatigue (consider a variation that avoids the pain), when a plateau persists despite progressive attempts on a given lift, or when equipment availability changes. Changing exercises too frequently prevents adaptation — the motor learning component of getting stronger at a lift takes 4–8 weeks to stabilize. Keep primary compound selections consistent for at least one full training block (4–8 weeks) before assessing whether a change is needed.

synthesis_text: Acute pain comes on suddenly, ties to a specific event or injury, and fades as healing progresses, functioning as a protective warning signal. Chronic pain, by contrast, persists for longer than three to six months and may continue even after the original injury has healed, because the nervous system keeps sending pain signals as if an emergency were still happening. Acute pain that does not resolve as expected can transition into chronic pain, though this is not inevitable. While acute pain from minor injuries typically responds well to conservative early care like rest and ice, chronic pain that has outlasted normal healing time warrants professional assessment.

expected.answer: Acute pain comes on suddenly, ties to a specific event or injury, and fades as healing progresses, functioning as a protective warning signal. Chronic pain, by contrast, persists for longer than three to six months and may continue even after the original injury has healed, because the nervous system keeps sending pain signals as if an emergency were still happening. Acute pain that does not resolve as expected can transition into chronic pain, though this is not inevitable. While acute pain from minor injuries typically responds well to conservative early care like rest and ice, chronic pain that has outlasted normal healing time warrants professional assessment.
